name: pypykatz (Pure Python Mimikatz)

auth.notes: Requires access to LSASS dump or memory; administrative privileges for live LSASS

performance.notes: Memory parsing speed; depends on dump size

agent_readiness.notes: Pure Python implementation of Mimikatz credential parsing. from pypykatz.pypykatz import pypykatz. mimi = pypykatz.parse_minidump_file('lsass.dmp'). for luid, session in mimi.logon_sessions.items(): print(session.username, session.domainname). creds = session.msv_creds[0]. cred.NThash, cred.LMHash, cred.username. SAM: pypykatz.parse_registry_files({'SYSTEM': 'SYSTEM', 'SAM': 'SAM'}). pypykatz.go_live() for live LSASS (Windows only, admin required). CLI: pypykatz lsa minidump lsass.dmp. pypykatz registry --sam SAM --system SYSTEM. Kerberos tickets: session.kerberos_creds. LSA secrets: session.lsa_credentials. Async support for live parsing.

gotchas: Authorized forensics and pentesting ONLY — anti-malware detection likely
Live LSASS access requires SYSTEM or local admin on Windows — high-risk operation
AV/EDR solutions will flag pypykatz as credential harvesting tool
Windows-specific credential formats — limited cross-platform credential types
No type annotations — poor IDE support; documentation sparse
best_when: Authorized Windows penetration testing for credential extraction from LSASS dumps
DFIR (Digital Forensics & Incident Response) analysis of Windows memory images
Red team operations with proper authorization — credential harvesting from captured systems
Security research on Windows credential storage mechanisms
avoid_when: Unauthorized systems — this is credential theft tooling
When Mimikatz itself is available and preferred by the engagement
summary: pypykatz is a pure Python Mimikatz implementation for Windows credential extraction. pypykatz.parse_minidump_file('lsass.dmp'). session.msv_creds[0].NThash. SAM/SYSTEM registry parsing. Kerberos tickets. LSA secrets. MIT by skelsec. FOR AUTHORIZED PENTESTING AND DFIR ONLY. AV/EDR will flag this. Live LSASS requires admin. No type annotations. Cross-platform (runs on Linux analyzing Windows dumps).
